meta.title: Polish My PR
meta.description: Prepare your PR for a smooth review and easy merge

items.text: Title is concise, descriptive, and follows team conventions (not "fix stuff" or "updates")

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR title should be concise, descriptive, and follow team conventions. Titles like 'fix stuff' or 'updates' tell reviewers nothing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The title is the first thing reviewers see and what appears in git log, release notes, and search results. A clear title helps reviewers prioritize and understand PRs at a glance.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the title. Can you tell what the PR does without reading the description? Does it follow the team's convention (e.g., 'feat: add user auth' or 'PROJ-123: Fix login timeout')?
baseContent.exampleComment: The title 'updates' doesn't describe the change. Something like 'Fix timeout in user authentication flow' would help reviewers understand what to expect.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Your PR title is a headline. Make it informative enough to understand without clicking through.

items.text: Description explains WHAT changed and WHY (not just HOW)

baseContent.whatItMeans: The description should explain what changed and why — the motivation, context, and design decisions. The code shows HOW; the description explains WHY.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reviewers need context to give meaningful feedback. Without knowing why a change was made, they can only review syntax, not whether the approach is correct.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the description. Do you understand the motivation? Could a new team member understand why this change exists?
baseContent.exampleComment: The description lists what files changed but not why. Adding context like 'Users were experiencing 5s timeouts during peak load because...' would help reviewers evaluate the approach.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Explain the problem before the solution. Reviewers who understand the 'why' give better feedback on the 'how.'

items.text: Link to ticket/issue is included

baseContent.whatItMeans: Include a link to the ticket, issue, or design document that motivated this change.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Links connect the code change to its business context. Future developers can trace from code to requirements. It also helps reviewers understand the full scope.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for a ticket/issue link in the description. Is it the right ticket? Does the ticket have enough context?
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you link the relevant Jira ticket? It would help me understand the full requirements and acceptance criteria.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always link to the ticket. It's the bridge between code and business context.

items.text: Screenshots or recordings included for UI changes

baseContent.whatItMeans: For UI changes, include screenshots or screen recordings showing the before and after states.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Visual changes are impossible to review from code alone. Screenshots let reviewers verify the UI matches designs and spot issues without checking out the branch.
baseContent.howToVerify: If the PR changes UI, are there screenshots? Do they show relevant states (default, loading, error, empty)? Before/after comparisons?
baseContent.exampleComment: This changes the login form layout but there are no screenshots. Could you add before/after images so we can verify it matches the design?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If it changes pixels, show pixels. Screenshots save reviewers from checking out your branch.

items.text: Impact and risk assessment stated for non-trivial changes

baseContent.whatItMeans: For non-trivial changes, state the impact and risk. What could go wrong? What's the blast radius? Is this easily reversible?
baseContent.whyItMatters: Impact assessment helps reviewers focus their attention on the riskiest parts and helps the team plan deployment timing and monitoring.
baseContent.howToVerify: For significant changes, is there a risk assessment? Does it mention what could go wrong, who's affected, and how to roll back?
baseContent.exampleComment: This changes the payment flow which affects all users. Could you add a risk assessment — what happens if the new validation rejects valid cards?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: State the risk upfront. It helps reviewers focus and the team plan deployment.

items.text: Breaking changes are called out prominently

baseContent.whatItMeans: If the PR introduces breaking changes (API changes, removed features, changed behavior), call them out prominently in the description.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Breaking changes that aren't called out get merged without downstream teams preparing. This leads to outages, broken clients, and emergency fixes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the PR change any public APIs, remove features, or alter existing behavior? Are these changes prominently documented?
baseContent.exampleComment: This renames the API response field from 'userId' to 'user_id' — that's a breaking change for all clients. Please add a 'BREAKING CHANGE' section to the description.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Breaking changes need a spotlight, not a footnote. Make them impossible to miss.

items.text: Migration or deployment notes included if applicable

baseContent.whatItMeans: If the change requires special deployment steps (database migrations, environment variables, feature flag setup), document them.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing deployment notes lead to failed deploys, missing config, and incidents. The deployer needs to know what's required beyond merging code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does this change require new env vars, migrations, or infrastructure changes? Are they documented with clear steps?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a new REDIS_URL environment variable but the deployment notes don't mention it. Ops won't know to configure it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If it needs more than 'merge and deploy,' write it down. Your future self at 2am will thank you.

items.text: PR is focused on a single logical change

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR should address one logical change — one feature, one bug fix, or one refactor. Not a mix of multiple concerns.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Multi-concern PRs are hard to review, hard to revert, and hard to understand in git history. If one part has issues, you can't merge the rest independently.
baseContent.howToVerify: Can you describe the PR's purpose in one sentence? Does it mix feature work with refactoring or cleanup? Could any part ship independently?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds the new API endpoint and refactors the auth middleware. Could we split these? The refactor can land first, then the endpoint builds on it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One concern per PR. If you need 'and' to describe it, it's probably too much.

items.text: No unrelated changes bundled in (drive-by fixes, formatting-only diffs)

baseContent.whatItMeans: Don't include drive-by fixes, formatting changes, or unrelated improvements alongside the main change.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unrelated changes obscure the actual diff, make git blame less useful, and complicate reviews. They also risk introducing bugs in code that wasn't the focus.
baseContent.howToVerify: Scan the file list. Are there files that don't relate to the PR's purpose? Are there formatting-only changes in otherwise untouched files?
baseContent.exampleComment: The linting fixes in these 5 files are unrelated to the auth changes. Could you move them to a separate cleanup PR?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep it focused. Save drive-by fixes for a dedicated cleanup PR.

items.text: PR is reasonably sized (ideally under ~400 lines of meaningful changes)

baseContent.whatItMeans: PRs should ideally be under ~400 lines of meaningful changes. Larger PRs get worse reviews and take longer to merge.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Research shows review quality drops sharply after 200-400 lines. Large PRs get rubber-stamped. Small PRs get thorough, fast reviews.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the diff size. Over 400 lines? Could it be split into logical chunks? Are there auto-generated files inflating the count?
baseContent.exampleComment: This is 1200 lines across 30 files. Breaking it into 3-4 focused PRs would get faster, higher-quality reviews.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Smaller PRs get better reviews, faster. If it's over 400 lines, consider splitting.

items.text: Large features are broken into stacked/sequenced PRs with clear ordering

baseContent.whatItMeans: Large features should be broken into stacked or sequenced PRs, each building on the previous, with a clear ordering and dependency chain.
baseContent.whyItMatters: One massive PR for a feature is unreviewable. Stacked PRs let reviewers focus on one piece at a time and provide earlier feedback.
baseContent.howToVerify: For large features, is there a plan for how the work is sequenced? Does each PR in the stack make sense on its own?
baseContent.exampleComment: This feature is big. Could we structure it as: PR1 (data model), PR2 (API), PR3 (UI)? Each would be independently reviewable.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Break big features into a PR stack. Each piece should be reviewable and mergeable on its own.

items.text: Refactoring is separated from behavior changes

baseContent.whatItMeans: Refactoring should be in separate PRs from behavior changes. Mixing them makes it impossible to verify that behavior didn't change.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When refactoring and behavior changes are mixed, reviewers can't tell if a difference is intentional (new feature) or structural (same behavior, different code).
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the PR mix restructuring with new functionality? Could the refactor be done first as a no-behavior-change PR?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR refactors the service layer and adds new validation logic. Splitting the refactor into its own PR would make both easier to review.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Refactor first, then build. Separate PRs make it clear what changed behavior and what didn't.

items.text: Commits are atomic — each builds and passes tests independently

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each commit should be a complete, working unit — it builds, tests pass, and represents one logical step.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Atomic commits enable git bisect for bug hunting, make reverts surgical, and tell a clear story of how the feature was built.
baseContent.howToVerify: Could you check out any individual commit and have a working build? Does each commit represent a complete thought?
baseContent.exampleComment: The 'add user model' commit doesn't compile without the 'add migrations' commit. Squashing them would make each commit independently valid.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every commit should compile and pass tests. If it doesn't stand alone, squash it.

items.text: Commit messages are descriptive and follow team conventions

baseContent.whatItMeans: Commit messages should follow team conventions (conventional commits, imperative mood) and explain what and why.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Good commit messages are the primary documentation in git log. They help future developers understand why changes were made months or years later.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the commit messages. Are they descriptive? Do they follow the project's conventions? Could you understand the change from the message alone?
baseContent.exampleComment: The commit messages are generic ('update files', 'fix bug'). Messages like 'fix: prevent duplicate form submission on slow networks' would be much more helpful in git log.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write commit messages for the person reading git log 6 months from now. That person might be you.

items.text: No "WIP", "fixup", "squash me" commits left in

baseContent.whatItMeans: Clean up work-in-progress commits before requesting review. No 'WIP', 'fixup', 'squash me', or 'temp' commits.
baseContent.whyItMatters: WIP commits are noise for reviewers and pollute git history. They signal the PR isn't ready and make the commit log useless.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the commit list for WIP, fixup, or placeholder commits. Have they been squashed into meaningful commits?
baseContent.exampleComment: There are 3 'WIP' commits and a 'fixup'. Could you squash these into the relevant feature commits before we review?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Clean your commits before requesting review. Interactive rebase is your friend.

items.text: Interactive rebase used to clean up history before requesting review

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use interactive rebase to organize commits into a logical sequence before requesting review. The history should tell a story.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A clean history makes the review process smoother and creates permanent documentation. Reviewers can follow the logical progression of changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the commit sequence tell a logical story? Is there a clear progression from setup to implementation to tests?
baseContent.exampleComment: The commit history jumps between the API and UI work. Grouping related commits together (all API changes, then all UI changes) would be easier to follow.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Organize commits like chapters in a story. The reviewer should be able to follow the progression.

items.text: I have read my own diff line by line in the GitHub UI

baseContent.whatItMeans: Before requesting review, read your own diff in the GitHub/GitLab UI — the same view reviewers will see.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reading in the review UI catches things you miss in your editor: leftover debug code, accidental file inclusions, and formatting issues. You'll spot 80% of your own mistakes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Have you looked at the diff in the PR UI? Did you check every file, including ones you didn't expect to change?
baseContent.exampleComment: There's a console.log on line 42 that looks like debug code. A self-review of the diff would have caught this.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Review your own PR first. You'll catch most issues before anyone else has to.

items.text: I have run the code locally and verified it works

baseContent.whatItMeans: Run the code locally and verify it works end-to-end before requesting review. Don't rely on CI alone.
baseContent.whyItMatters: CI catches build and test failures but not whether the feature actually works as intended. Manual verification catches integration issues and UX problems.
baseContent.howToVerify: Have you run the application and tested the changed feature? Did you test the happy path and at least one error path?
baseContent.exampleComment: This form validation change looks correct in the code, but have you verified it works in the browser? The error messages might not display correctly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you haven't run it, you haven't tested it. CI is necessary but not sufficient.

items.text: Removed all debug statements (console.log, fmt.Println, System.out.println, print())

baseContent.whatItMeans: Remove all debug statements before requesting review: console.log, fmt.Println, System.out.println, print(), debugger statements.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Debug code in production clutters logs, leaks internal information, and looks unprofessional. It also shows the PR wasn't self-reviewed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for console.log, fmt.Println, print(, debugger, System.out, and other debug patterns.
baseContent.exampleComment: There are several console.log statements left in the diff. Could you clean these up before we review the logic?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Search your diff for debug output. Remove it all before requesting review.

items.text: No commented-out code left in — deleted, not commented

baseContent.whatItMeans: Delete commented-out code. Don't leave it in 'just in case' — version control is your backup.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Commented code creates confusion: Is it temporary? A reference? Will it be re-enabled? It's noise that makes reviews harder and code harder to read.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for large blocks of commented-out code. Is any of it intentionally preserved, or is it leftover from development?
baseContent.exampleComment: This commented-out function has been replaced by the new implementation. Since git preserves history, we can safely delete it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Delete dead code. Git remembers so you don't have to.

items.text: No accidental file inclusions (.env, IDE configs, large binaries, node_modules)

baseContent.whatItMeans: Check that no accidental files are included: .env files, IDE configs (.idea/, .vscode/), large binaries, node_modules, build artifacts.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Accidental files bloat the repo, potentially expose secrets, and waste reviewer time. Large binary files can't be easily removed from git history.
baseContent.howToVerify: Review the file list. Are there files that shouldn't be committed? Is .gitignore up to date?
baseContent.exampleComment: There's a .env file in the diff that contains the database URL. This should be in .gitignore, not committed.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Check the file list before submitting. One .env file can ruin your day.

items.text: No secrets or credentials committed (API keys, passwords, tokens)

baseContent.whatItMeans: Verify no secrets, credentials, API keys, passwords, or tokens are in the code or commit history.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Secrets in git history are almost impossible to fully remove. Even if you delete them in a later commit, they persist in history. Once pushed, they must be rotated.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for common secret patterns: API_KEY, password, token, secret, Bearer, aws_access_key. Check .env files and config files.
baseContent.exampleComment: This config file contains what looks like a production API key. Please remove it and rotate the key, as it's now in git history.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Secrets in git are permanent. Check before you push. Rotate if you find them.

items.text: Checked for typos in code, comments, and strings

baseContent.whatItMeans: Check for typos in code, comments, strings, and variable names. They're easy to fix now and embarrassing to fix later.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Typos in user-facing strings look unprofessional. Typos in variable names make code harder to search. Typos in comments mislead future developers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read through your diff looking specifically for misspellings. Check user-facing strings, error messages, and comments.
baseContent.exampleComment: Small typo: 'recieve' should be 'receive' on line 42. Also 'sucessful' should be 'successful' in the error message.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: A quick spell-check of your diff takes 2 minutes and prevents embarrassing corrections later.

items.text: New code has corresponding unit tests

baseContent.whatItMeans: New code should have corresponding tests that verify its behavior.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untested code is a liability. Tests document expected behavior, catch regressions, and give confidence for future changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are there new test files or test cases for the new functionality? Do they cover the important behaviors?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new utility function doesn't have any tests. Adding tests now, while the context is fresh, will save time later.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write tests with your code, not after. The context is freshest now.

items.text: All existing tests still pass

baseContent.whatItMeans: All existing tests must still pass. Your change should not break anything that was working before.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Broken tests block other developers and indicate your change has unintended side effects. Fixing them now is cheaper than debugging later.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run the full test suite locally. Do all tests pass? Are there test failures related to your changes?
baseContent.exampleComment: The test suite shows 3 failing tests in the user module. These seem related to your auth changes. Could you investigate and fix them?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Green tests are a prerequisite for review. Fix failures before requesting review.

items.text: Edge cases are covered (empty inputs, null, error paths, boundary values)

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should cover edge cases: empty inputs, null values, boundary values, error paths, and unusual but valid states.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Happy path tests catch obvious bugs. Edge case tests catch the subtle bugs that make it to production and cause incidents at 3am.
baseContent.howToVerify: Review test cases. Are there tests for empty arrays, null inputs, zero values, large inputs, and error conditions?
baseContent.exampleComment: The tests cover the normal flow but not edge cases. What happens when the input array is empty? When the user has no permissions?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test the edges: empty, one, many, zero, null, max. That's where production bugs hide.

items.text: Test names are descriptive and document expected behavior

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test names should describe the expected behavior in plain language, forming a readable specification when listed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When a test fails, the name is your first clue. 'test1 failed' helps nobody. 'should return 404 when user not found' tells you exactly what broke.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the test names. Could someone unfamiliar with the code understand what's being tested?
baseContent.exampleComment: The test 'it works' doesn't describe the behavior. Something like 'returns sorted results when sort=ascending' would be clearer.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test names are documentation. Write them so a failure message tells you what broke.

items.text: No flaky tests introduced (no timing dependencies, no ordering reliance)

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should not depend on timing, execution order, or external state. They should pass reliably every time.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Flaky tests erode trust in the test suite. Developers start ignoring failures, and real bugs slip through.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do tests use sleep/timeout for timing? Do they depend on other tests running first? Do they depend on network or external services?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test uses a 2-second sleep to wait for an async operation. Use a proper wait mechanism to avoid flaky failures in CI.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: A flaky test is worse than no test. It teaches the team to ignore failures.

items.text: Integration tests added for cross-module or API changes

baseContent.whatItMeans: For changes that cross module boundaries or affect APIs, add integration tests that verify the components work together.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unit tests verify individual pieces. Integration tests verify they work together. Many bugs live in the seams between components.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does this change affect how modules interact? Are there integration tests covering the interaction points?
baseContent.exampleComment: This changes the API response format, but the integration tests still test the old format. They need updating to verify the new contract.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Unit tests for pieces, integration tests for seams. Both are needed for cross-module changes.

items.text: Public API changes have updated documentation

baseContent.whatItMeans: If you changed a public API (REST endpoints, library functions, SDK methods), update the corresponding documentation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Outdated API docs cause integration bugs and support burden. Consumers build against the docs, not the code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does this PR change any public-facing API? Are the docs (OpenAPI, JSDoc, README, wiki) updated to match?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a new query parameter to the search endpoint but the OpenAPI spec doesn't reflect it. Frontend developers won't know about it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you change the API, update the docs. Consumers rely on docs, not source code.

items.text: README updated if setup or usage changed

baseContent.whatItMeans: If the PR changes setup steps, environment variables, or running instructions, update the README.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An outdated README wastes hours of new developer time. If setup requires a step the README doesn't mention, nobody can get started.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does this change add new dependencies, env vars, or setup steps? Is the README updated?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds Redis as a dependency but the README still says 'just run npm start.' New developers will get a confusing error.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you changed how to set up or run the project, update the README.

items.text: Inline comments added for non-obvious or complex logic

baseContent.whatItMeans: Add comments for non-obvious logic, workarounds, and business rules. Don't comment what the code does — comment why it does it.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Self-documenting code explains what. Comments explain why. A workaround without a comment becomes mysterious legacy code that nobody dares to touch.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are there complex algorithms, business rules, or workarounds without explanation? Would a new developer understand why this code exists?
baseContent.exampleComment: This retry with a 37-second delay is oddly specific. A comment explaining why 37 seconds (e.g., 'matches the upstream cache TTL') would prevent someone from 'fixing' it later.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Comment the why, not the what. Future developers will thank you for explaining the weird parts.

items.text: Changelog updated if project maintains one

baseContent.whatItMeans: If the project maintains a changelog, update it with a summary of user-facing changes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Changelogs communicate what changed to users, stakeholders, and other teams. They're the bridge between code changes and release communication.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the project have a CHANGELOG.md or equivalent? Does this PR introduce user-facing changes that should be noted?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a new export feature that users will see. Could you add an entry to the changelog under 'Added'?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Changelog for user-facing changes. Keep it updated as you go, not at release time.

items.text: Assigned appropriate reviewers (domain experts, CODEOWNERS)

baseContent.whatItMeans: Assign reviewers who have relevant domain knowledge. Check CODEOWNERS if the project has one.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Domain experts catch subtle issues that generalists miss. The right reviewer for a database migration is different from the right reviewer for a CSS change.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are the assigned reviewers familiar with this area of the codebase? Does the CODEOWNERS file specify required reviewers?
baseContent.exampleComment: This changes the payment flow — could you add someone from the payments team as a reviewer? They'd catch edge cases I might miss.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: The right reviewers find the right issues. Choose based on expertise, not availability.

items.text: Added helpful inline comments on complex sections of the diff

baseContent.whatItMeans: Add inline comments on your own PR to guide reviewers through complex sections, explain non-obvious decisions, or highlight areas of concern.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Self-comments save reviewer time and focus attention where it matters. They show you've thought about the code and make the review conversation more productive.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are there complex sections that would benefit from a self-comment? Have you explained any non-obvious design decisions?
baseContent.exampleComment: Consider adding a self-comment on the new caching logic explaining why you chose LRU over TTL. It would preempt the question reviewers will have.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Comment your own PR. Guide reviewers to what matters and explain the non-obvious.

items.text: Provided testing instructions or reproduction steps

baseContent.whatItMeans: Provide clear instructions for how to test the change manually, including setup steps and expected results.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reviewers who can reproduce and test the change give better feedback. Without instructions, they review only the code, not whether it works.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are there testing instructions? Could a reviewer follow them to verify the change works?
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you add testing instructions? Something like: '1. Run the app, 2. Navigate to /settings, 3. Click export, 4. Verify the PDF downloads.'
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Make it easy for reviewers to test your change. Clear instructions = better feedback.

items.text: Labeled the PR appropriately (size, area, priority)

baseContent.whatItMeans: Label the PR with relevant tags: size (S/M/L), area (frontend/backend/infra), priority, and type (feature/bugfix/chore).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Labels help reviewers prioritize and triage. A small bug fix can be reviewed quickly; a large feature needs dedicated time.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are appropriate labels applied? Does the team have a labeling convention? Is the size label accurate?
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you add a size label? This helps reviewers plan their time — a 'size/L' PR needs a dedicated review slot.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Labels help reviewers prioritize. A quick label saves everyone's time.

items.text: Set draft status if not ready for final review

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use draft status when the PR isn't ready for final review but you want early feedback or CI results.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Draft PRs set expectations. Reviewers won't waste time doing a thorough review on code that's still changing. When you mark it ready, they know to invest time.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is the PR in draft status while still in progress? Is it marked ready when actually ready for review?
baseContent.exampleComment: This is still a work in progress based on the TODO comments. Consider marking it as a draft until it's ready for review.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Draft means 'not ready, but look if you want.' Ready means 'please review.' Use them correctly.

items.text: CI pipeline passes (lint, build, tests)

baseContent.whatItMeans: The CI pipeline (lint, build, tests) should pass before requesting review. Fix failures, don't ignore them.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A failing CI wastes reviewer time — they might review code that won't even compile. Green CI is a baseline for review readiness.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is the CI pipeline green? Are there any failures or warnings?
baseContent.exampleComment: CI is showing a test failure in the auth module. Could you fix that before we review? It's hard to evaluate the change when the baseline is broken.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Green CI before review. Don't waste reviewer time on code that doesn't build.

items.text: No new lint warnings or compiler warnings introduced

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR should not introduce new lint warnings, compiler warnings, or deprecation notices. Fix them proactively.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Warnings accumulate into a wall of noise that hides real issues. Each warning ignored makes the next one easier to ignore.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the linter and compiler output. Are there new warnings compared to the base branch? Are deprecation warnings addressed?
baseContent.exampleComment: The build shows 3 new deprecation warnings from this change. Could you address them now before they accumulate?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Zero new warnings. Treat warnings as errors and fix them immediately.

items.text: Dependencies are locked/pinned (lockfile updated)

baseContent.whatItMeans: Dependencies should be locked via a lockfile (package-lock.json, yarn.lock, go.sum). The lockfile should be committed and up to date.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without locked dependencies, different environments install different versions, causing 'works on my machine' bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is the lockfile committed? Is it updated to reflect any new dependencies? Are there conflicting lockfiles?
baseContent.exampleComment: The package-lock.json wasn't updated after adding the new dependency. Run `npm install` and commit the updated lockfile.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Commit your lockfile. It's the source of truth for dependency versions.

items.text: Database migrations are reversible and tested

baseContent.whatItMeans: Database migrations should include a down/rollback migration so the change can be reverted if something goes wrong.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Irreversible migrations mean you can't rollback a deployment. If the new code is broken, you're stuck with a database schema that the old code can't use.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the migration have both up and down scripts? Does the down migration actually reverse the changes?
baseContent.exampleComment: This migration adds a column but the down migration is empty. If we need to rollback, the old code will see an unexpected column.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every migration needs a working rollback. Test the rollback, not just the migration.

items.text: Feature flags in place for risky changes that need gradual rollout

baseContent.whatItMeans: For risky changes, use feature flags to control rollout. This decouples deployment from release.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Feature flags let you deploy code without exposing it, roll out to a subset of users, and instantly disable a feature if issues arise.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is this a risky change? Would a feature flag allow safer rollout? Can it be turned off without a deployment?
baseContent.exampleComment: This changes the payment flow for all users at once. A feature flag would let us roll out gradually and revert instantly if there are issues.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Feature flags for risky changes. Deploy anytime, release safely.
